reason_for_reviewarrears.reasons_for_review_arrears:
Over the last 18 months my household has experienced a combination of reduced income and increased essential expenses, which together have made it increasingly difficult to keep up with the full contractual mortgage repayments on time.

Up until mid-2023, I (Michael Patrick O’Connor) had a very stable income as a Senior Network Systems Engineer with Columbus Health Systems, and my wife, Laura, worked part-time as a freelance graphic designer while also caring for our two children (currently ages 9 and 6). We were consistently paying the full mortgage amount of approximately €1,785 per month (equivalent in USD from our U.S. bank) without any issues.

However, in August 2023, my employer went through a restructuring of the IT department. While I was not laid off, my role was adjusted and my overtime/on-call opportunities were significantly reduced. Previously, overtime and on-call allowances regularly contributed an additional 10–15% to my annual income. After the restructuring, that additional income dropped to almost zero. In practical terms, this meant our household lost roughly €700–€900 per month in variable income that we had previously relied on to comfortably cover both the mortgage and other obligations such as childcare, car payments, and medical co-pays.

At almost the same time, our daughter, Emma (age 9), was diagnosed with mild scoliosis and required a series of specialist consultations, imaging, and custom orthotic supports. While we do have health insurance, the out-of-pocket costs and co-pays were higher than we anticipated. Between September 2023 and March 2024, we paid approximately €3,000 equivalent in medical expenses directly related to her diagnosis and treatment plan. These expenses were not optional and had to take priority over some other bills.

Additionally, our younger son, Aidan (age 6), started full-day school in fall 2023. While this eventually reduced childcare costs slightly, in the short term we had overlapping expenses: we were still paying for part-time after-school care, school supplies, and some additional transportation and activity costs. Our monthly essential outgoings (food, utilities, fuel, and children’s needs) increased by about €200–€300 compared with the previous year, partly due to general inflation and rising utility bills.

Because of these changes, from around October 2023 onward, we began to fall slightly behind on the mortgage. Initially, the shortfall was only one or two hundred euro per month, but over time it accumulated into a more noticeable arrears balance. We tried to manage by cutting back on discretionary spending (restaurants, vacations, and non-essential subscriptions), but the combination of reduced overtime income and increased medical and household costs meant that we still could not consistently reach the full contractual repayment amount each month.

In early 2024, we also had an unexpected major car repair. Our 2015 Honda CR-V, which we rely on for my commute and the children’s school drop-offs, needed a new transmission component and brake work. The total bill was just under €1,800 equivalent. We had to put part of this on a credit card because we did not have sufficient savings left after paying for medical bills and regular monthly expenses. The new credit card payment further strained our monthly budget and contributed indirectly to the mortgage arrears, because we had to choose between paying down the credit card (to keep it from going into default) and paying the full mortgage amount.

It is important to emphasize that we have not simply stopped paying; we have continued to make regular payments every month, but they have sometimes been partial or slightly late. For example, there were a few months where we could only pay about €1,300–€1,400 toward the mortgage instead of the full €1,785, and we typically caught up as much as possible the following month once my base salary and any small bonuses came through. This pattern, however, has led to a gradual build-up of arrears rather than an immediate, single missed payment.

We are now seeking a review and possible restructuring of the mortgage so that the monthly repayment amount is more manageable relative to our current and foreseeable income. My base salary remains stable, and my employment with Columbus Health Systems is secure (I have been there since 2013 and have consistently positive performance reviews). My wife is also gradually increasing her freelance work now that both children are in school full-time, which we expect will improve our income over the next 12–24 months. In the meantime, we are asking for temporary relief or a modification (such as extending the mortgage term or temporarily reducing payments) so that we can clear the arrears in an orderly way without risking default.

To summarize the main reasons for the review and arrears:
1. Reduction in variable income due to loss of overtime and on-call shifts following IT department restructuring in August 2023.
2. Significant unplanned medical expenses for our daughter’s scoliosis diagnosis and ongoing treatment.
3. Increased essential household costs (utilities, food, school-related expenses) due to inflation and both children being in school with related fees and activities.
4. An unexpected major car repair in early 2024, partly financed by credit card, which added another mandatory monthly payment.

We are committed to keeping our home and resolving the arrears. We are willing to provide any supporting documentation (pay stubs, medical bills, repair invoices, bank statements) to substantiate the above and to work collaboratively on a sustainable repayment arrangement that reflects our actual financial capacity while allowing us to gradually bring the account back up to date.
